sentences.text: Yes.
Yes.
It was [INAUDIBLE], street number three.
It was really the beginning of the ghetto part of Warsaw.
It was a very strange world for me.
I was born in my grandmother's-- maternal grandmother's apartment in the center of town.
We lived there the first few years of my life.
Then we lived next door to my paternal grandmother's apartment, also in the center of town.
And moving there, into [INAUDIBLE], was a-- such a different, strange world that I'd never seen before.
I never knew I belonged there.
I didn't really.
I didn't speak Yiddish.
And the street there was a Yiddish-speaking street.
And all those strangely dressed men in black coats and with beards, it was a world I'd never known before.
The flat was small and not very well-equipped.
But I understood then that we had to-- that had to sort of economize.
And we had to go into a poorer part of town.
I could understand that much.
But I still could not find any connecting-- any connecting points with the world I'd been thrown into.
Next door but one-- but one from that building was a Catholic church.
And our nanny, Marynia-- she did not live with us then.
We couldn't afford a nanny or the house help-- would come sometimes and take me there.
But this world was just as strange as the prayer house world.
I didn't belong to either of them at all.
No, they didn't.
They-- as far as I know, they didn't.
I just think that it didn't make any difference to them.
They did not worry about me becoming religious.
Because I just wasn't brought up that way.
That was not in my makeup.
They never saw any danger in it.
And I guess they were right.
We lived there for about two years.
And that was my first and only contact with a really Jewish world in Warsaw, until the war.
Then when things improved, we moved into Mokotowska, number three.
We had a lovely flat there, three room, on the ground floor, in the back part of the flat.
So we didn't face the street, but the yard.
And there we loved living.
It was almost the end of this part of town.
And next to us was vacant-- vacant land that was used sometimes as a horse-racing ground.
That was later, during the war, converted into little vegetable gardens.
So this part was really nice, except for the character that was stopping me in the street there.
Because that's where it had happened to me.
I give a lot of thought to those early-- early years of mine.
I don't know whether my parents were right, bringing us up this way.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
I found it that maybe if we were enlightened as to our origins, as to our belonging, maybe it wouldn't be such a shock to find out what we were, what Hitler wanted-- wanted us to be.
Having a Jewish grandmother and grandfather, even if they were converted, still made us Jewish.
And there was nothing to alter that fact.
I don't think I was really interested in anything like that.
And we-- we-- it was not a topic we talked about at home.
I did not actually meet anti-Semitism at school, either of the schools I went to.
So that these were the only instances.
And I don't think it was ever a topic spoken about at home, to any extent.
Very much so.
Yes.
Very much so.
We woke up to the sound of bombs falling on Warsaw.
We-- actually, we were expecting something to happen for some months.
And that year, first September is the end of school holidays in Poland.
It's the day children go back to school.
That summer my mother rented a house very close to Warsaw.
Because there was fear that something could happen.
And we'd just come back from the holidays.
And we were all ready for the war.
We thought there was something coming.
So we had gas masks.
And we all thought there would be a gas war, like in World War I.
And we woke up to the sound of bombs falling on Warsaw.
It was a shock.
But it was something, those first few days, of a bit of an adventure.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I had a very good friend that last-- last year, who I thought I was madly in love with, at my ripe age of 12.
And I thought, all right, whatever happens, whenever he goes, I go.
And that's it.
Let's be brave and fight our wars together.
On the 6 September, the German army was advancing on Warsaw.
There was an appeal by the city president for all men able to carry arms to leave the city and go east, to regroup there and be mobilized.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] My father's office had closed in the first couple of days of war.
His boss managed to go back to-- to France.
He wanted my father to go.
But that was already impossible.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Father got six months' salary as a sort of one-off payment.
And that's about the extent of what we were left with.
So on the 6 September, father was leaving Warsaw.
And my brother Piotr wanted to go with him.
He was, as I said, big, well built.
He looked 18 more than 14.
And Mother was in tears.
She didn't want to let him go.
She was terrified to lose him.
And he was crying.
Mom, you don't love me.
Do you want the Germans to catch me?
Why don't you want to let me go?
So finally, she agreed.
And they both left that  day.
We never saw them again.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
I might come back later to whatever happened to them.
But I don't think that my mother had ever forgiven herself for letting him go.
I remember that Piotr was not circumcised.
He didn't look Jewish either.
But the first thing the Germans did, if they suspected anyone, any boy or man, was to pull his pants down.
And mother felt that she could have saved him, as she saved me.
But they'd-- they'd gone.
So out of that six months' salary, they took half.
And we got the other half.
And that's about what we had for the years ahead of us.
Well, the first thing we did-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there were trenches being dug just outside of the flats where we lived.
Because that was sort of the edge of town.
And there was fear that there'll be fighting there.
So we moved into the center, to my grandmother's.
And we stayed there throughout most of the siege of Warsaw.
We stayed in the-- in her upstairs flat first.
Then we moved into the cellars.
When the bombardment became very heavy, we moved into the cellars.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then when it became even heavier, we moved into my Aunt [?
Marisha's ?] flat, which was in another area of the center of town.
And we stayed there until Warsaw was surrendered, which was towards the end of September.
Some three weeks.
We had a dog.
We had a dog.
When my father's boss went to France, he left him his dog, a little dachshund.
And she was a lovely little bitch.
But she was going crazy with the bombardment, with the noise.
We had taken her to the cellars.
But all the neighbors were protesting, this is no place for dogs.
It's for humans only.
So we ended up having to leave her upstairs in the flat.
She was going totally berserk.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Life in the cellars, Warsaw was running out of water.
We had to be bringing water from taps in the street.
The streets were under constant bombardment.
We were running out of food.
Mother had to queue to get a bit of bread, a tin of condensed milk, anything at all to feed us.
My grandma refused to come down to the cellar.
She said, I am too old to do that.
I'm staying in my bed until an artillery shell went right through the walls of her bedroom above her head.
Next time, she'd gone down to the cellar.
There was her and my mother and I, and the [INAUDIBLE].
There were also lots of neighbors.
We were all living there.
They were not cellars built as shelters.
They were built as [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] to keep vegetables for winter, potatoes, onions, coal.
They were not meant to shelter people.
But we had no choice, really.
We had to sit there.
Mother was the only person venturing outside to bring some water or whatever food she could find.
I was terrified every time she'd gone out.
She wouldn't let me go with her.
Somehow we survived Warsaw bombardment.
We'd survive, later, a lot worse bombardments.
Then we thought it was the end of the world.
But obviously it wasn't.
Then we moved into my aunt's flat.
And the bombardment became even heavier and worse.
But that was only two or three days, and then eerie quiet.
The city had surrendered.
We also had, during the siege, continuous soldiers going through.
The retreating army, they were dirty, with terrible shoes falling off their feet.
They were hungry and thirsty and often wounded.
And we had to help them, wash them, maybe then give them some water.
It was really sort of a difficult time.
But by, I think, the 26 September, it was all over.
The first-- the first siege, the first bombardment of the war was finished.
Then we went back to our flat in Mokotowska.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] About a month later, my uncle, Anna's husband, in Lodz, he was a member of the Bund.
He was a city councilor.
He was arrested in October in the first group of hostages in Lodz, and shot soon after, executed soon after.
And then my aunt and grandma, who lived the last few years before the war in Lodz.
And aunt's two children-- Alina, who was my senior of about four years, and [?
Jannick ?] who was my junior of a year and a half-- came to Warsaw.
And they moved into our flat.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It was a terrible winter, that first war winter.
It was exceedingly cold.
There was no coal.
There was no way of heating the flat.
There was little food.
We had not all that much money.
We couldn't buy much on the black market.
There were continuous [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] German soldiers coming to check whether we've given away all the radio sets.
And radio sets were forbidden from the [? get go. ?] There were German soldiers coming to check for guns.
We knew that our father, my father, had had a gun.
But we didn't know whether he took it with him or whether he'd left it.
We were terrified that they'd find it in the flat.
We didn't even know where to look for it.
Sometime later we found it, in the pocket of his coat.
And we just threw it out with the rubbish heap.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] That first year of war, there was no school.
There was-- there were no legal high schools under the German occupation.
So my mother and my aunt sort of did some schoolwork with us, as much as that was possible at home.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] That was winter, '39, '40.
Financially, we were not all that well off.
Came summer, mother organized us making ice cream and selling it through the window.
Because we lived on the ground floor.
And people around sort of very quickly learned that there was ice cream to be had there.
And they'd come to buy it from us.
It was great fun for us.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
What else do I remember from those times?
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Really little.
We had news that Father and my brother arrived in Vilna, where there was still my grandmother's family.
And they stayed there.
It was occupied.
The whole east of Poland had been occupied by Russia.
But you could then correspond between the two occupation zones.
We knew they were there.
Mother wanted them to come back.
But they somehow couldn't make up their minds.
Or maybe they tried and couldn't.
We never really knew for sure what was happening.
But they never did come back.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] That state of affairs lasted for the first year, until October 1940, when we all had to move into the ghetto.
The ghetto was, in the meantime, surrounded by a three-meter-high wall with barbed wire on top.
And October 30 was the deadline for all Jews to move into the ghetto area.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Aunt Anna had moved with her two children into a separate flat.
Grandma, my maternal grandma, and mother and I moved into a small two-room flat in [INAUDIBLE] street.
It was a huge building with a multitude of small flats.
Half of that building belonged to my grandmother.
So she found a place for us there.
But we're the three of us there, plus my paternal uncle, Joseph, who moved with us because he couldn't find another place in the ghetto.
Four in two small rooms, it was not much fun living there.
Right from the beginning, problems of food supply started.
We didn't have all that much money.
If Grandma was wealthier, her money was probably invested and not available to be spent on food.
Food supply was extremely immediate.
We had rations that were very, very, scant.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] For some months, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Mother-- we were supplementing our income.
A baker would bring a big basket of bread rolls to us.
And I was taking that basket and sending the bread rolls for breakfast into flats of people we knew.
I mean, with all that poverty, and lack of food in the ghetto, there were still fresh bread rolls every morning, which is quite amazing.
I don't know how much money we'd made on that.
I know that I was continuously hungry and I would, every day, eat one or two rolls on the way and tell my mother that the baker had made a mistake, that he'd [INAUDIBLE].
My mother was making some additional money-- as I found out one day being nosy a little bit-- by selling contraceptives among her friends.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It wasn't easy.
We'd buy-- we'd get a ration of bread for four people.
And we had to cut it into four pieces amongst my grandmother, my uncle, and the two of us.
And they were all looking, whose piece is just a little bit bigger than the other one?
We'd get two potatoes.
And we had to cook them and-- and divide them into four servings.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] A lot of the food was, come winter, frozen and hardly possible to eat, like frozen potatoes, frozen pumpkin.
That was quite a monstrosity.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] On the other hand, there were some reasonably good times.
School, as I said, was strictly forbidden, under death sentence.
But there were, underground, illegal schools operating throughout Warsaw.
And the same in the ghetto.
And there I went to a school called [INAUDIBLE].
That was originally, before the war, a boys-only school.
But in the ghetto, they-- they had girls' groups and boys' groups.
We had groups of no more than five or six meeting in private flats.
There were six girls in the group I went to.
Out of them, I know one only, besides myself, that survived.
She's gone to the states after the war.
We had normal secondary school classes.
Very strict teachers, and we're trying to make it-- teachers were trying to make it as normal as possible.
I remember the girl who survived was called Hania Kohn.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There was Wanda Rechthand who was a gorgeous girl, with a very strong character.
I'll come back to her later.
There was Hala Piwnic.
I don't remember the other two.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] The other thing we were doing, there was operating in the ghetto, a society called the Society of Advancing Agriculture Amongst Jews.
And we occupied a vacant block of land outside the hospital or something.
And we dug out the concrete.
And we were planting flowers.
And we were also breeding chicks-- chickens in an incubator.
It was really more fun than anything else.
But we-- it kept us busy.
One day, one of the girls picked a rose out of the flowers that we'd planted, and took it home.
Next day, somebody sent her a note-- a Polish rhyme, saying-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] don't pick roses, Annie, said mother.
Roses are prickly.
You'll cut your fingers.
And roses are prickly.
Annie picked a rose.
She pricked her finger and cried.
It's really hard to translate into English.
But that's what it meant.
It was a little rhyme.
And the girl who picked the rose answered with another rhyme, saying, let's not mourn roses when the forests are burning.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So we had some-- we spent some time in that [INAUDIBLE] so-called garden.
Other than that, life in the ghetto was something terrifying and extremely strange.
The Germans herded something like a quarter of a million Jews from outlying towns and villages into Warsaw, into the Warsaw ghetto.
And all those people had nowhere to go, nowhere to live, nothing to eat.
The streets were always crowded.
You just couldn't walk a street without brushing against people.
It was impossible.
They were all indescribably dirty, bedraggled, hungry.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Lice were a continuous problem.
Typhus was rampant.
And it was impossible to avoid lice.
It was enough to brush against someone in the street, to get them.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Little boys who had-- who starving took to snatching purses that people were carrying, and putting things into their mouths right away, hoping that was food.
Well, sometimes it was.
Sometimes it happened that things that they thought was jam was a tooth-- shoe-- shoe polish, or whatever.
There were other children begging, going around yards, singing songs and begging.
They were about the only Yiddish words that I learned there.
